lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Good morning.
lines.translation: 早上好。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Safe House Insurance.
lines.translation: 安居保险公司。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: My name is Paul.
lines.translation: 我叫保罗。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: How can I help you today?
lines.translation: 今天我能为您做些什么？

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: Morning.
lines.translation: 早上好。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: I wouldn’t say that it’s good from where I am standing.
lines.translation: 从我现在的处境来看，我可不觉得这个早晨有什么好。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: This is Miss Wilson, and this is the third time I’ve called this week since receiving your letter about our insurance claim.
lines.translation: 我是威尔逊小姐，这是我本周第三次打电话了，自从收到你们关于我们保险索赔的来信之后。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: I’m getting a little fed up with my calls about my claim being completely disregarded.
lines.translation: 我有点受够了，我关于索赔的电话一直被完全忽视。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Miss Wilson, thank you for calling back.
lines.translation: 威尔逊小姐，感谢您再次来电。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Can I take some details to help me look at your claim?
lines.translation: 我可以记录一些信息，帮我查看您的索赔吗？

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: It’s Miss May Wilson of 15 South Sea Road in Cornwall, and the details are that our village was extensively flooded two months ago.
lines.translation: 我是住在康沃尔南海路15号的梅·威尔逊小姐，具体情况是，我们村两个月前遭遇了严重洪灾。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: The entire ground floor of our cottage was submerged in water, and five of us have been living in a caravan ever since.
lines.translation: 我们小屋的一楼全被水淹了，从那以后我们五个人一直住在一辆房车里。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: You people are still withholding the money we are entitled to over a bizarre technical detail.
lines.translation: 你们仍然因为一个荒唐的技术细节，扣着我们有权获得的钱。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: And it’s not acceptable, Paul!
lines.translation: 这不能接受，保罗！

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Miss Wilson, according to the notes on your account, the bizarre technical detail that you mentioned refers to the fact that you hadn’t paid house insurance the month before the incident.
lines.translation: 威尔逊小姐，根据您账户上的记录，您提到的那个荒唐的技术细节是指，事故发生前一个月您没有缴纳房屋保险费。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: That money left our account.
lines.translation: 那笔钱已经从我们的账户里扣走了。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: And now that you should be paying out, you are suddenly saying that you didn’t receive it on time.
lines.translation: 现在到了你们该赔付的时候，你们却突然说没有按时收到那笔钱。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: I’m really skeptical about this claim.
lines.translation: 我真的很怀疑这种说法。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: The contract does say that any missed payment in a year will affect the terms and conditions of the insurance contract and may affect claims.
lines.translation: 合同确实写明，一年中任何一次漏缴都会影响保险合同的条款和条件，并可能影响索赔。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Of course, I can pass you on to my manager to talk to you more about this.
lines.translation: 当然，我可以把您转给我的经理，让他和您进一步谈这件事。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: I’ve already spoken to him, and you can tell him I’m furious now, and that your company has a lawsuit on its hands.
lines.translation: 我已经和他说过了，你可以告诉他，我现在非常愤怒，而且你们公司要吃官司了。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: You will be hearing from my lawyer.
lines.translation: 我的律师会联系你们的。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: Goodbye.
lines.translation: 再见。

lines.speaker: Q1.
lines.text: What is the woman complaining about?
lines.translation: 这位女士在抱怨什么？

lines.speaker: Q2.
lines.text: What is the problem the woman’s family encountered?
lines.translation: 这位女士的家人遇到了什么问题？

lines.speaker: Q3.
lines.text: What has caused the so-called “bizarre technical detail” according to the man?
lines.translation: 根据男士所说，所谓“荒唐的技术细节”是由什么造成的？

lines.speaker: Q4.
lines.text: What does the woman say she would do at the end of the conversation?
lines.translation: 谈话结束时，这位女士说她会做什么？

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: How do you feel about the future of artificial intelligence?
lines.translation: 你怎么看人工智能的未来？

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: Personally, I feel quite optimistic about it.
lines.translation: 就我个人而言，我对此相当乐观。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: AI?
lines.translation: 人工智能？

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: I’m not so optimistic actually.
lines.translation: 实际上我没那么乐观。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: In fact, it’s something we should be concerned about.
lines.translation: 事实上，这是我们应该担心的事情。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: Well, it will help us humans understand ourselves better.
lines.translation: 嗯，它会帮助我们人类更好地了解自己。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: And, when we have a better understanding of ourselves, we can improve the world.
lines.translation: 而且，当我们更好地了解自己时，我们就能改善世界。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Well, one thing is for sure: Technology is evolving faster than our ability to understand it.
lines.translation: 嗯，有一点是肯定的：技术发展的速度比我们理解它的能力要快。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: And in the future, AI will make jobs kind of pointless.
lines.translation: 将来，人工智能会让工作变得有点没有意义。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: I think artificial intelligence will actually help create new kinds of jobs, which would require less of our time and allow us to be centered on creative tasks.
lines.translation: 我认为人工智能实际上会帮助创造新的工作类型，这些工作会占用我们更少的时间，让我们把精力集中在创造性任务上。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: I doubt that very much.
lines.translation: 我很怀疑这一点。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Probably the last job that we will remain will be writing AI software.
lines.translation: 也许最后剩下的工作会是编写人工智能软件。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: And then eventually, AI will just write its own software.
lines.translation: 然后最终，人工智能会自己编写软件。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: At that time, we are going to have a lot of jobs which nobody will want to do, so we will need artificial intelligence for the robots to take care of the old guys, like us.
lines.translation: 到那时，会有很多没人愿意做的工作，所以我们会需要人工智能，让机器人照顾像我们这样的老人。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: I don’t know.
lines.translation: 我不知道。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: There’s a risk that human civilization could be replaced by a superior type of digital life.
lines.translation: 人类文明有可能被一种更高级的数字生命形式取代。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: AI will be able to completely simulate a person in every way possible.
lines.translation: 人工智能将能够以各种可能的方式完全模拟一个人。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: In fact, some people think we’re in the simulation, right now.
lines.translation: 事实上，有些人认为我们现在就处在模拟世界中。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: That’s impossible.
lines.translation: 那不可能。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: Humans can’t even make a mosquito.
lines.translation: 人类甚至连一只蚊子都造不出来。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: Computers only have chips.
lines.translation: 电脑只有芯片。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: People have brains, and that’s where the wisdom comes from.
lines.translation: 人有大脑，而智慧正是由此而来。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Once it’s fully developed, AI will become tired of trying to communicate with humans, as we would be much slower thinkers in comparison.
lines.translation: 一旦人工智能完全发展起来，它会厌倦试图与人类交流，因为相比之下，我们的思维速度会慢得多。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: I’m not so sure.
lines.translation: 我不太确定。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: A computer is a computer, and a computer is just a toy.
lines.translation: 电脑就是电脑，电脑只不过是个玩具。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: Computers can easily communicate incredibly fast.
lines.translation: 电脑可以非常快速地轻松交流。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: So the computer will just get impatient talking to humans.
lines.translation: 所以电脑和人类交谈时会变得不耐烦。

lines.speaker: M:
lines.text: It’ll be barely getting any information out.
lines.translation: 它几乎获取不到什么信息。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: Well, I believe there’s a benevolent future with AI.
lines.translation: 嗯，我相信人工智能会带来一个善意的未来。

lines.speaker: W:
lines.text: I also think you watch too many science fiction films.
lines.translation: 我还觉得你看了太多科幻电影。

lines.speaker: Q5.
lines.text: What do we learn about the speakers from the conversation?
lines.translation: 关于两位说话者，我们从对话中了解到什么？

lines.speaker: Q6.
lines.text: What will new kinds of jobs be like, according to the woman?
lines.translation: 根据这位女士的说法，新型工作会是什么样的？

lines.speaker: Q7.
lines.text: What is the risk the man anticipates?
lines.translation: 这位男士预见到了什么风险？

lines.speaker: Q8.
lines.text: What is the man’s concern about AI technology?
lines.translation: 这位男士对人工智能技术有什么担忧？

lines.text: To achieve financial security, how much you save is always more important than the amount you earn or how shrewdly you invest.
lines.translation: 要实现财务安全，你存多少钱始终比你赚多少钱或投资得多精明更重要。

lines.text: If you’re under 30 years old, your goal should be to save 20% of your monthly income after tax deductions.
lines.translation: 如果你不到30岁，你的目标应该是在扣税后每月收入中存下20%。

lines.text: This is irrespective of how much you earn.
lines.translation: 这与你赚多少钱无关。

lines.text: Approximately 50% should be reserved for essentials like food and accommodation.
lines.translation: 大约50%应该留给食物和住宿等必需品。

lines.text: The remaining 30% is for recreation and entertainment.
lines.translation: 剩下的30%用于休闲和娱乐。

lines.text: But for many young people, it'll be difficult to designate such a large proportion of their income for savings.
lines.translation: 但对许多年轻人来说，把这么大比例的收入指定为储蓄会很困难。

lines.text: If you find it hard to save any money at all, start by cutting all unnecessary spending.
lines.translation: 如果你发现自己根本很难存下钱，就先削减所有不必要的开支。

lines.text: Allocate a tiny amount of 1 or 2 percent for savings and gradually increase that amount.
lines.translation: 先拿出1%或2%这样很小的一部分用于储蓄，然后逐步增加这个数额。

lines.text: Always keep that 20% goal in mind to prevents yourself from becoming complacent.
lines.translation: 始终牢记20%这个目标，以防自己变得自满。

lines.text: It can be challenging to stick to such a strict plan.
lines.translation: 坚持这样严格的计划可能很有挑战性。

lines.text: But if you adopt the right mindset, you should be apt to make it work for you.
lines.translation: 但如果你采取正确的心态，你应该能够让它为你所用。

lines.text: So, what should you be doing with the money that you're saving?
lines.translation: 那么，你应该如何处理自己存下来的钱呢？

lines.text: Some must be kept easily accessible in case you need some cash in an emergency.
lines.translation: 一部分必须保持容易取用，以备紧急时需要现金。

lines.text: The largest proportion should be invested in retirement plans, either through your employer or privately.
lines.translation: 最大的一部分应该投入退休计划，可以通过雇主，也可以私人投资。

lines.text: And you can keep some money for high-risk but potentially lucrative investments.
lines.translation: 你也可以留一些钱用于高风险但可能获利丰厚的投资。

lines.text: Dividends can be reinvested or used to purchase something you like.
lines.translation: 股息可以再投资，也可以用来购买你喜欢的东西。

lines.text: By following this plan, you should hopefully be able to enjoy your life now, and still be financially secure in the future.
lines.translation: 按照这个计划，你应该有望既能享受现在的生活，又能在未来保持财务安全。

lines.speaker: Q9.
lines.text: What are people under 30 advised to do to achieve financial security?
lines.translation: 为了实现财务安全，30岁以下的人被建议做什么？

lines.speaker: Q10.
lines.text: What should people do if they find it difficult to follow the speaker’s advice on their financial plan?
lines.translation: 如果人们发现很难遵循说话者关于财务计划的建议，他们应该怎么做？

lines.speaker: Q11.
lines.text: What does he speaker think is important for achieving financial security?
lines.translation: 说话者认为实现财务安全重要的是什么？

lines.text: I work in advertising and I like to keep up with current trends, mainly because I’m aware that we live in an image-obsessed world.
lines.translation: 我在广告行业工作，我喜欢跟上当前潮流，主要是因为我意识到我们生活在一个痴迷形象的世界里。

lines.text: However, when I first started my job, occasionally, I'd catch a glimpse of myself in the lifts and find myself thinking that I looked a total mess.
lines.translation: 然而，当我刚开始工作时，偶尔会在电梯里瞥见自己，然后发现自己在想，我看起来真是一团糟。

lines.text: Was I being held back by my choice of clothing?
lines.translation: 我是不是被自己的穿衣选择拖了后腿？

lines.text: The short answer is yes, especially when clients are quick to judge you on your style rather than your work.
lines.translation: 简短的答案是肯定的，尤其是当客户很快根据你的风格而不是你的工作来评判你时。

lines.text: But no one can be unique with their outfit every day.
lines.translation: 但没有人能每天都穿得独一无二。

lines.text: I mean that’s why uniforms were invented.
lines.translation: 我的意思是，这就是制服被发明出来的原因。

lines.text: So, here’s what I did.
lines.translation: 所以，我是这样做的。

lines.text: I created my own uniform.
lines.translation: 我创造了自己的制服。

lines.text: To do this, I chose an appropriate outfit.
lines.translation: 为此，我选择了一套合适的装束。

lines.text: Then I bought multiple items of the same style in different shades.
lines.translation: 然后我买了多件同一款式、不同色调的衣服。

lines.text: Now, I never worry about what I’m wearing in the morning even if I do get a bit tired of just wearing the same classic pieces.
lines.translation: 现在，早上我再也不用担心穿什么了，即使我确实有点厌倦了总是穿同样的经典单品。

lines.text: Overall, when it comes to work, you have to ask yourself: “Will looking smarter enhance my ability to do my job?
lines.translation: 总的来说，说到工作，你必须问自己：“穿得更得体会增强我做工作的能力吗？

lines.text: ” For some, this question may not be an issue at all, especially if you work remotely and rarely see your colleagues or clients face to face.
lines.translation: ” 对一些人来说，这个问题可能根本不是问题，尤其是如果你远程工作，很少与同事或客户面对面见面。

lines.text: But if your job involves interacting with other people, the answer to this is often yes.
lines.translation: 但如果你的工作需要与他人互动，这个问题的答案通常是肯定的。

lines.text: So rather than fighting the system, I think we should just do whatever helps us to achieve our goals at work.
lines.translation: 所以，与其对抗这个体系，我认为我们应该做任何有助于我们在工作中实现目标的事情。

lines.text: If that means playing it safe with your image, then let’s face it.
lines.translation: 如果这意味着在形象上稳妥一点，那我们就面对现实吧。

lines.text: It’s probably worth it.
lines.translation: 这很可能是值得的。

lines.speaker: Q12.
lines.text: What do we learn about the speaker when she first started her job?
lines.translation: 关于说话者刚开始工作时的情况，我们了解到什么？

lines.speaker: Q13.
lines.text: Why were uniforms invented according to the speaker?
lines.translation: 根据说话者所说，制服为什么被发明出来？

lines.speaker: Q14.
lines.text: What does the speaker say about looking smarter?
lines.translation: 说话者关于穿得更得体说了什么？

lines.speaker: Q15.
lines.text: What does the speaker advise people to do in an image-obsessed world?
lines.translation: 在一个痴迷形象的世界里，说话者建议人们怎么做？

lines.text: Did you know that Americans have approximately three times the amount of space we had 50 years ago?
lines.translation: 你知道吗，美国人现在拥有的空间大约是50年前的三倍？

lines.text: Therefore, you’d think we’d have sufficient room for all of our possessions.
lines.translation: 因此，你可能会认为我们有足够的空间放下所有物品。

lines.text: On the contrary, the personal storage business is now a growing industry.
lines.translation: 恰恰相反，个人仓储业务如今成了一个不断增长的行业。

lines.text: We've got triple the space, but we’ve become such enthusiastic consumers that we require even more.
lines.translation: 我们的空间增加到了三倍，但我们变成了如此热衷消费的人，以至于还需要更多空间。

lines.text: This phenomenon has resulted in significant credit card debt, enormous environmental footprints, and perhaps not coincidentally, our happiness levels have failed to increase over the same half century.
lines.translation: 这种现象导致了大量信用卡债务、巨大的环境足迹，也许并非巧合的是，在同样的半个世纪里，我们的幸福水平并没有提高。

lines.text: I’m here to suggest an alternative that having less might actually be a preferable decision.
lines.translation: 我在这里想提出一种替代选择：拥有更少东西实际上可能是更可取的决定。

lines.text: Many of us have experienced, at some stage, the pleasure of possessing less.
lines.translation: 我们许多人在人生的某个阶段都体验过拥有更少东西的快乐。

lines.text: I propose that less stuff and less space can not only help you economize, but also simplify your life.
lines.translation: 我认为，更少的物品和更小的空间不仅可以帮助你省钱，还能简化你的生活。

lines.text: I recently started an innovative project to discover some creative solutions that offered me everything I required.
lines.translation: 我最近启动了一个创新项目，想找到一些有创意的解决方案，为我提供我所需要的一切。

lines.text: By purchasing an apartment that was 40 square meters, instead of 60, I immediately saved $200,000.
lines.translation: 通过购买一套40平方米而不是60平方米的公寓，我立刻节省了20万美元。

lines.text: Smaller space leads to reduced utility bills, and also a smaller carbon footprint.
lines.translation: 更小的空间会带来更低的水电费，也会带来更小的碳足迹。

lines.text: Because it is designed around an edited collection of possessions, limited to my favorite stuff, I'm really excited to live there.
lines.translation: 因为它是围绕一组经过精简的物品设计的，只限于我最喜欢的东西，所以我真的很期待住在那里。

lines.text: How can we live more basically?
lines.translation: 我们怎样才能生活得更简单？

lines.text: Firstly, we must ruthlessly cut the unnecessary objects out of our lives.
lines.translation: 首先，我们必须无情地把生活中不必要的物品清除出去。

lines.text: To stem consumption, we should think before we buy and ask ourselves, “Will it truly make me happier?
lines.translation: 为了遏制消费，我们应该在购买前思考，并问自己：“它真的会让我更快乐吗？

lines.text: ” Obviously, we should possess some great stuff.
lines.translation: ” 显然，我们应该拥有一些很棒的东西。

lines.text: But we want belongings that we’re going to love for years.
lines.translation: 但我们想要的是那些我们会喜爱多年的物品。

lines.text: Secondly, we require space efficiency.
lines.translation: 其次，我们需要空间效率。

lines.text: We want appliances that are designed for use most of the time, not for occasional use.
lines.translation: 我们想要的是大部分时间都会使用的电器，而不是偶尔才用的电器。

lines.text: Why own a six-burner stove when you rarely use even three burners?
lines.translation: 如果你连三个炉头都很少用到，为什么要拥有一个六炉头的炉灶呢？

lines.text: Finally, we need multifunctional spaces and housewares.
lines.translation: 最后，我们需要多功能空间和家居用品。

lines.text: I combined a moveable wall with transforming furniture to get more out of my limited space.
lines.translation: 我把一面可移动墙和变形家具结合起来，从有限的空间中获得更多用途。

lines.text: Consider my coffee table.
lines.translation: 以我的咖啡桌为例。

lines.text: It increases in size to accommodate ten.
lines.translation: 它可以增大到能容纳十个人。

lines.text: My office is tucked away, easily hidden.
lines.translation: 我的办公室被收纳起来，很容易隐藏。

lines.text: My bed simply pops out of the wall.
lines.translation: 我的床可以直接从墙里弹出来。

lines.text: For guests, I can relocate the moveable wall and utilize the foldable guest beds I installed.
lines.translation: 有客人来时，我可以移动那面可移动墙，并使用我安装的折叠客床。

lines.text: I’m not saying we should all live in tiny apartments, but consider the benefits of an edited life.
lines.translation: 我不是说我们都应该住在小公寓里，但请考虑一下精简生活的好处。

lines.text: When you return home and walk through your front door, take a moment to ask yourself, “Could I do with a little life editing?
lines.translation: 当你回到家、走进前门时，花点时间问问自己：“我是否可以稍微整理一下生活？

lines.text: Would that give me more freedom and more time?
lines.translation: 那会给我更多自由和更多时间吗？

lines.speaker: Q16.
lines.text: What has prevened Americans' happiness levels from increasing?
lines.translation: 是什么阻碍了美国人幸福水平的提高？

lines.speaker: Q17.
lines.text: What things should we possess according to the speaker?
lines.translation: 根据说话者所说，我们应该拥有什么样的东西？

lines.speaker: Q18.
lines.text: What do we learn about the items in the speaker's home?
lines.translation: 关于说话者家中的物品，我们了解到什么？

lines.text: Now, believe it or not, people sometimes lie in order to maintain a good, honest reputation—even if it hurts them to do so.
lines.translation: 现在，信不信由你，人们有时会为了保持良好、诚实的声誉而撒谎，即使这样做会伤害他们自己。

lines.text: At least, this is what a team of scientists is suggesting, with evidence to prove it.
lines.translation: 至少，这是一组科学家根据证据提出的观点。

lines.text: Picture this scenario: You often drive for work and can be compensated for up to 400 miles per month.
lines.translation: 想象这样一个情景：你经常因工作开车，每月最多可以报销400英里的里程。

lines.text: Most people at your company drive about 300 miles each month.
lines.translation: 你公司里大多数人每月大约开300英里。

lines.text: But this month, you drove 400 miles.
lines.translation: 但这个月，你开了400英里。

lines.text: How many miles do you think you'd claim in your expense report?
lines.translation: 你觉得你会在费用报告中申报多少英里？

lines.text: The scientists asked this exact question as part of the study we’re discussing today.
lines.translation: 科学家们在我们今天讨论的研究中问了这个确切的问题。

lines.text: With surprising results, they found that 12% of respondents reported the distance they drove as less than the actual figure, giving an average answer of 384 miles.
lines.translation: 结果令人惊讶，他们发现12%的受访者报告的行驶距离低于实际数字，平均答案为384英里。

lines.text: In other words, they lied about the number of miles, even though they would forfeit money they were owed.
lines.translation: 换句话说，他们在里程数上撒了谎，尽管这样会放弃本该属于他们的钱。

lines.text: The researchers believe this was to seem honest, with the assumption being that others would be suspicious of a high expense claim.
lines.translation: 研究人员认为，这是为了显得诚实，因为他们假设别人会怀疑过高的费用报销。

lines.text: But why would people fabricate numbers to their own detriment?
lines.translation: 但为什么人们会编造对自己不利的数字呢？

lines.text: The researchers explain that many people care a great deal about their reputation and how they'll be judged by others.
lines.translation: 研究人员解释说，许多人非常在意自己的声誉以及别人会如何评价他们。

lines.text: If they care enough, their concern about appearing honest and not losing the respect of others may be greater than their desire to actually be honest.
lines.translation: 如果他们足够在意，那么他们对显得诚实、不失去他人尊重的担忧，可能会超过他们真正诚实的愿望。

lines.text: The researchers assert that the findings suggest that when people obtain very favorable outcomes, they anticipate other people’s suspicious reactions and prefer lying and appearing honest to telling the truth and appearing as selfish liars.
lines.translation: 研究人员断言，这些发现表明，当人们获得非常有利的结果时，他们会预料到他人的怀疑反应，并宁愿撒谎、显得诚实，也不愿说真话却显得像自私的骗子。

lines.text: So why is this research important?
lines.translation: 那么，为什么这项研究很重要？

lines.text: Well, experts generally agree there are two main types of lie: selfish lies and lies that are meant to benefit others.
lines.translation: 嗯，专家们通常认为谎言主要有两种：自私的谎言和旨在让他人受益的谎言。

lines.text: The first, as you may predict, is for selfish gain, such as submitting a fraudulent claim to an insurance company, while the second involves lying to help others or not offend others, for example, telling a friend whose outfit you don’t like that they look great.
lines.translation: 第一种，正如你可能预料的，是为了自私的利益，比如向保险公司提交欺诈性索赔；第二种则涉及为了帮助他人或不冒犯他人而撒谎，例如告诉一个你不喜欢其穿搭的朋友她看起来很棒。

lines.text: But the researchers are suggesting a third type of lying—lying to maintain a good reputation.
lines.translation: 但研究人员提出了第三种撒谎类型，即为了维持良好声誉而撒谎。

lines.text: Now this hypothesis is new and some skeptics argue that this isn’t a whole new category of lie.
lines.translation: 现在这个假设是新的，一些怀疑者认为这并不是一种全新的谎言类别。

lines.text: But the findings seem intuitive to me.
lines.translation: 但这些发现对我来说似乎很符合直觉。

lines.text: After all, one of the main motivations for lying is to increase our worth in the eyes of others, so it seems highly likely that people will lie to seem honest.
lines.translation: 毕竟，撒谎的主要动机之一是提升我们在他人眼中的价值，所以人们为了显得诚实而撒谎似乎很有可能。

lines.speaker: Q19.
lines.text: What did a team of scientists find in their study?
lines.translation: 一组科学家在他们的研究中发现了什么？

lines.speaker: Q20.
lines.text: Why would people fabricate numbers to their own detriment according to the researchers?
lines.translation: 根据研究人员的说法，为什么人们会编造对自己不利的数字？

lines.speaker: Q21.
lines.text: What does the speaker think of the researchers’ findings?
lines.translation: 说话者如何看待研究人员的发现？

lines.text: Why do old people dislike new music?
lines.translation: 为什么老年人不喜欢新音乐？

lines.text: As I’ve grown older, I often hear people my age say things like “They just don’t make good music like they used to.
lines.translation: 随着年龄增长，我经常听到我这个年龄的人说这样的话：“他们再也做不出以前那样的好音乐了。

lines.text: ” Why does this happen?
lines.translation: ” 为什么会发生这种情况？

lines.text: Luckily, psychology can give us some insights into this puzzle.
lines.translation: 幸运的是，心理学可以让我们对这个谜题有一些认识。

lines.text: Musical tastes begin to crystallize as early as age 13 or 14.
lines.translation: 音乐品味早在13岁或14岁时就开始定型。

lines.text: By the time we’re in our early 20s, these tastes get locked into place pretty firmly.
lines.translation: 到我们20岁出头时，这些品味已经相当牢固地固定下来。

lines.text: In fact, studies have found that by the time we turn 33, most of us have stopped listening to new music.
lines.translation: 事实上，研究发现，到我们33岁时，大多数人已经停止听新音乐了。

lines.text: Meanwhile, popular songs released when you’re in your early teens are likely to remain quite popular among your age group through the rest of your life.
lines.translation: 与此同时，在你十几岁早期发布的流行歌曲，可能会在你余生中一直深受你这个年龄群体的喜爱。

lines.text: There could be a biological explanation for this, as there is evidence that the brain’s ability to make subtle distinctions between different chords, rhythms and melodies deteriorates with age.
lines.translation: 这可能有生物学上的解释，因为有证据表明，随着年龄增长，大脑区分不同和弦、节奏和旋律细微差别的能力会退化。

lines.text: So to older people, newer, less familiar songs might all “sound the same.
lines.translation: 所以对老年人来说，较新的、不太熟悉的歌曲可能全都“听起来一样。

lines.text: But there may be some simpler reasons for older people’s aversion to newer music.
lines.translation: 但老年人厌恶新音乐可能还有一些更简单的原因。

lines.text: One of the most researched laws of social psychology is something called the “mere exposure effect,” which, in essence, means that the more we're exposed to something, the more we tend to like it.
lines.translation: 社会心理学中研究最多的法则之一叫作“单纯曝光效应”，其本质是，我们接触某样东西越多，就越倾向于喜欢它。

lines.text: This happens with people we know, the advertisements we see and the songs we listen to.
lines.translation: 这会发生在我们认识的人、我们看到的广告以及我们听的歌曲上。

lines.text: When you’re in your early teens, you probably spend a fair amount of time listening to music or watching music videos.
lines.translation: 当你十几岁早期时，你可能会花相当多时间听音乐或看音乐视频。

lines.text: Your favorite songs and artists become familiar, comforting parts of your routine.
lines.translation: 你最喜欢的歌曲和艺术家会成为你日常生活中熟悉而令人安心的一部分。

lines.text: For many people over 30, job and family obligations increase, so there’s less time to spend discovering new music.
lines.translation: 对许多30岁以上的人来说，工作和家庭责任增加，因此用于发现新音乐的时间更少。

lines.text: Instead, many will simply listen to old, familiar favorites from that period of their lives when they had more free time.
lines.translation: 相反，许多人只会听那些来自人生中更有空闲时间阶段的、熟悉的旧爱。

lines.text: Of course those teen years weren’t necessarily carefree.
lines.translation: 当然，青少年时期并不一定无忧无虑。

lines.text: They’re famously confusing, which is why so many TV shows and movies revolve around high school turmoil.
lines.translation: 那段时期以令人困惑著称，这也是为什么这么多电视剧和电影都围绕高中时期的混乱展开。

lines.text: Psychology research has shown that the emotions that we experience as teens seem more intense than those that come later.
lines.translation: 心理学研究表明，我们在青少年时期经历的情绪似乎比之后的情绪更强烈。

lines.text: And we also know that intense emotions are associated with stronger memories and preferences.
lines.translation: 我们也知道，强烈的情绪与更深刻的记忆和偏好有关。

lines.text: Both of these might explain why the songs we listen to during this period become so memorable and beloved.
lines.translation: 这两点或许可以解释，为什么我们在这一时期听的歌曲会变得如此难忘且深受喜爱。

lines.text: So there’s nothing wrong with your parents because they don’t like your music.
lines.translation: 所以，你的父母不喜欢你的音乐并没有什么问题。

lines.text: Rather it’s all part of the natural order of things.
lines.translation: 相反，这都是事物自然规律的一部分。

lines.speaker: Q22.
lines.text: What does the speaker mainly discites in this talk?
lines.translation: 说话者在这次讲话中主要讨论了什么？

lines.speaker: Q23.
lines.text: What have studies found about most people by the time they turn 33?
lines.translation: 研究发现，大多数人到33岁时会怎样？

lines.speaker: Q24.
lines.text: What do we learn from one of the most researched laws of social psychology?
lines.translation: 我们从社会心理学中研究最多的法则之一了解到什么？

lines.speaker: Q25.
lines.text: What might explain the fact that songs people listen to in their teen years are memorable and beloved?
lines.translation: 什么可能解释了人们在青少年时期听的歌曲令人难忘且深受喜爱这一事实？
